title: Podman --tmpfs is not `noexec` by default
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
According to the documentation here: http://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html#tmpfs-fs

When using `--tmpfs` the default mount options should be `rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev.`

However, this seems to be incorrect, as the default ones from my tests result in: 

```
    {
      "destination": "/run",
      "type": "tmpfs",
      "source": "tmpfs",
      "options": [
        "rw",
        "rprivate",
        "nosuid",
        "nodev",
        "tmpcopyup"
      ]
    },
 ```
 
 These options here ^ are also the default mount options used for `tmpfs` automatically created for systemd. 
 Specifically, /run, /tmp and /var/tmp. 
 
 I am wondering if this is a documentation issue or bug? 
 
 
**Lastly**, if I am interested in have `noexec` by default do I have any options? 
Or should I simply create all the mount points myself? 

i.e.

```
 --read-only-tmpfs=false --tmpfs /run:rw,noexec,nosuid,rprivate,nodev,tmpcopyup  --tmpfs /tmp:rw,noexec,nosuid,rprivate,nodev,tmpcopyup  --tmpfs /vat/tmp:rw,noexec,nosuid,rprivate,nodev,tmpcopyup
```

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.

```
sudo podman --storage-driver=vfs create --read-only -it --name test--rootfs /data/rootfs /bin/bash
```

2.

```
sudo podman --storage-driver=vfs start -ia  test
```

3.
Results in config.json with mount point not including `noexec`

**Also**
```
sudo podman --storage-driver=vfs create --read-only -it --read-only-tmpfs=false --tmpfs /run --rootfs /data/rootfs /bin/bash
```

Results in the same behavior as above. 



**Describe the results you received:**
/run mounted with `exec`

**Describe the results you expected:**
I would expect `run` to be mounted as `noexec` according to documentation. 


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.3.0-dev

```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
podman info --debug
host:
  arch: arm64
  buildahVersion: 1.21.1
  cgroupControllers:
  - cpuset
  - cpu

  - blkio

  - hugetlb

  - debug
  cgroupManager: cgroupfs
  cgroupVersion: v1
  conmon:
    package: 'conmon: /usr/local/bin/conmon'
    path: /usr/local/bin/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 2.0.29-dev, commit: '
  cpus: 8
  distribution:
    distribution: ubuntu
    version: "18.04"
  eventLogger: file
  hostname: pan-appliance
  idMappings:
    gidmap: null
    uidmap: null
  kernel: 4.9.140
  linkmode: dynamic
  memFree: 24989339648
  memTotal: 33424560128
  ociRuntime:
    name: runc
    package: 'runc: /usr/sbin/runc'
    path: /usr/sbin/runc
    version: 'runc version spec: 1.0.1-dev'
  os: linux
  remoteSocket:
    path: /run/podman/podman.sock
  security:
    apparmorEnabled: true
    capabilities: CAP_AUDIT_WRITE,CAP_CHOWN,CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE,CAP_FOWNER,CAP_FSETID,CAP_KILL,CAP_MKNOD,CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE,CAP_NET_RAW,CAP_SETFCAP,CAP_SETGID,CAP_SETPCAP,CAP_SETUID,CAP_SYS_CHROOT
    rootless: false
    seccompEnabled: true
    seccompProfilePath: /usr/share/containers/seccomp.json
    selinuxEnabled: false
  serviceIsRemote: false
  slirp4netns:
    executable: ""
    package: ""
    version: ""
  swapFree: 16712269824
  swapTotal: 16712269824
  uptime: 49h 50m 32.94s (Approximately 2.04 days)
registries:
  search:
  - docker.io
  - registry.fedoraproject.org
  - registry.access.redhat.com
store:
  configFile: /etc/containers/storage.conf

    number: 0

    Backing Filesystem: extfs

    Supports d_type: "true"

  imageStore:
    number: 0
  runRoot: /run/containers/storage

  APIVersion: 3.3.0-dev

```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
Built podman myself for Ubuntu 18.04
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md)**


No

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Physical

comments.body: /run should definitely have noexec. That is the only one that is definitely wrong. rprivate and tmpcopyup are needed.
I remember we originally had noexec and had to revert because Docker doesn't do it and people were accustomed to sticking things in `/tmp` and running them.

Ok lets fix the man page.

@KCSesh Interested in opening a PR to fix this?

So this means my best option if I want `noexec` is to manually mount the `systemd` mounts myself right? 

`
--tmpfs /run:rw,noexec,nosuid,rprivate,nodev,tmpcopyup  --tmpfs /tmp:rw,noexec,nosuid,rprivate,nodev,tmpcopyup  --tmpfs /var/tmp:rw,noexec,nosuid,rprivate,nodev,tmpcopyup
`

And I would probably add: 
` --read-only-tmpfs=false`

For additional sanity. 

And yes I can create a PR!

I guess this is a good time to ask about the `systemd` mounts as well. 
The [documentation](http://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html#systemd-true-false-always) says: 

```
/run
/run/lock
/tmp
/sys/fs/cgroup/systemd
/var/lib/journal
```

But in my config.json, I only notice: 

```
/run
/tmp
```

And then the others like:

```
/var/tmp
/dev/mqueue
/dev/pts
/proc
/sys
/dev
/run/.containerenv
/sys/fs/cgroup
```

And of course the network bind mounts:

```
/etc/hostname
/etc/hosts
```


Edit:

I think I understand, it is due to this paragraph:

`The value always enforces the systemd mode is enforced without looking at the executable name. Otherwise, if set to true and the command you are running inside the container is systemd, /usr/sbin/init, /sbin/init or /usr/local/sbin/init.`

Hmmm, I guess I need to test to see the mount options for these new directories.

Looks like some `systemd` mounts **do not have** `noexec` as well. 


```
    {
      "destination": "/var/log/journal",
      "type": "tmpfs",
      "source": "tmpfs",
      "options": [
        "rw",
        "rprivate",
        "nosuid",
        "nodev",
        "tmpcopyup"
      ]
    },
```

```
    {
      "destination": "/run/lock",
      "type": "tmpfs",
      "source": "tmpfs",
      "options": [
        "rw",
        "rprivate",
        "nosuid",
        "nodev",
        "tmpcopyup"
      ]
    },
```
`/tmp` and `run` of course look the same as above. 


But `/sys/fs/cgroup/systemd` does have `noexec`.

```
 {
      "destination": "/sys/fs/cgroup/systemd",
      "type": "bind",
      "source": "/sys/fs/cgroup/systemd",
      "options": [
        "bind",
        "rprivate",
        "nodev",
        "noexec",
        "nosuid"
      ]
    }
```

So this is kind of odd - 
I think if I want `noexec` as **default** and the ability to support systemd containers, I need to set:

`--systemd=always` 

Then I need to make sure I manually create the mount points with `noexec`: 

```
/run
/run/lock
/tmp
/sys/fs/cgroup/systemd
/var/lib/journal
```

I would also add: `/var/tmp`with `noexec` 

Is this what is expected @rhatdan and @mheon  ?

So because of this, I think I always have to mount the systemd directories if I want to support systemd (even if it is not used) if I want this abstracted from the users. Which I need to consider if I turn it on always or just turn it off...

I think what would be best is if I have a way to tell podman my default mount options for `tmpfs` that way I can keep systemd on true, instead of always. And have confidence that any new mounts podman adds will always be `noexec` since I will have defined it as a default. 

Is this something that exists? @rhatdan and @mheon

Also, another note: `/dev` 

`dev` is mounted as 

```
    {
      "destination": "/dev",
      "type": "tmpfs",
      "source": "tmpfs",
      "options": [
        "nosuid",
        "strictatime",
        "mode=755",
        "size=65536k"
      ]
    },
```

I am curious if it is safe to set `noexec` on `/dev` and if there is a reason it was not added? 
Or why these values were picked? 

(Again having default tmpfs options I think would be ideal) 


**Update:**

I tried setting `/dev` as a manual `--tmpfs` and ran into an error:

```
container_linux.go:349: starting container process caused "process_linux.go:449: container init caused \"open /dev/ptmx: no such file or directory\"": OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found
```

So I guess `/dev` needs to have `exec`? 
I am trying to make my container more secure by adding the `--read-only` flags and making sure all mount points are `noexec` but is this not possible with `/dev` ?


**Update 2:**
If I don't use the interactive psudeo-tty then I can successfully start the container. I think that makes sense since ptmx is related to the psudeo terminal: https://linux.die.net/man/4/ptmx

I can run the container with `noexec` mount option on `/dev` which is nice. 

However, I won't have `podman exec` access to the container ever, if I went this route.

I guess to give my questions a TLDR, since a lot occurred above: 

1. Is there an option in podman I can set, to give the default values for `tmpfs`? Or do I need to manually set all tmpfs individually all appending `noexec` where I need it? 
i. Specifically for Systemd, without the options to default tmpfs options, I need to essentially pick starting containers as `systemd=always` or `systemd=false` because systemd mount points are also not `noexec` and this is the mount option I am looking to add. 
      a. Unless I could have conditional arguments for podman. If systemd is used, then mount `/var/logs/jounal` as `noexec` etc. 

2. `/dev/` seems pretty unique as a `tmpfs` is there any security implications from leaving `/dev` mounted with `exec`? 
I find it odd that `noexec` is usually suggested to make mount points more secure, but then leaving a mount point `exec` seems counter intuitive. Any comments on this? 
   i. Seems I can make it `noexec` but I don't understand the implication of this in total. I can't access the container with `podman exec` or use `-it` runtime options, which is not a deal breaker, because I am launching containers as a service. But will I be able to attach and use a GPU? (need to test this) 
   

Looking forward to your insights @rhatdan and @mheon

We attempt to match the host settings for /dev and other file systems.  /dev on your host is not mounted noexec.
There is no default way to set tmpfs mounts at this point, but theoretically it could be added to containers.conf

Interested in opening a PR?
@rhatdan do you see any glaring issues with `dev` being noexec other than issues with psudeo terminal?

Actually I might open a new issue looking into `/dev` it seems to be a bit odd the behavior. 

I can't seem to successfully create a tmpfs mount at this location using the `--tmpfs` option. 
Even if I use the same exact mount options that it defaults to. 

Update:
Created: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/11343

I know that /dev right now is using devtmpfs rather then tmpfs, Not sure what the difference is.  I have no idea on noexec on /dev, but it makes sense that is should be set.  It was probably not thought of, since /dev is usually only writable by Root.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I think we can close this issue.  Mounting tmpfs as `noexec` is a breaking change and it is possible to specify `:noexec` when it is the desired behavior.
I have actually been moving forward on mounting /dev as noexec.
